query: us housing market 2021 boom why prices

videos.title: Real estate boom: Mortgage rates at all-time-lows and high demand are what's driving up prices: NAR
videos.text: the housing market remains red hot as home prices grew by the double digits in metro areas now that's all according to a report from the national association of realtors so let's discuss that we're joined now by lawrence u national association of realtors chief economist great to have you here with us lawrence i feel like i get this question a lot and so i'm going to pose it to you as so many folks out there right now are trying to buy a home and finding that frankly a lot of them are unaffordable they they can't afford some of these home prices and the biggest question i always get is why so can you explain to us right now why we are seeing some of these home prices growing as the report is finding by the double digits in some cities well thanks for having me on the show the past 12 months has been quite exceptional nothing like this in terms of 20 percent price appreciation in a single year and this is happening in ninety percent of the metro markets so it's not a one or two city phenomena it is a nationwide phenomenon why well the mortgage rate hitting all-time low so it was simply too good to pass up for some home buyers but we have to remember we had a housing shortage before the pandemic and the shoreditch simply exacerbated during the pandemic so you had a strong demand lack of supply well what popped out was home prices now that's a great news for homeowners but a tremendous challenge for potential first-time buyers well and then i want to follow up on that if home prices are so elevated we've seen some of the numbers for new sale new home sales existing sales we've seen those come off their peaks by quite a wide margin is it simply the case that high home excuse me high housing prices are the cure for high housing prices and then where do you see these numbers leveling off in the future well uh the price increase is again due to the housing shortage but because of the price increases now it is deterring buyers especially the first time buyers who have to come up with fresh down payment now the trade-off buyers have no problem with that down payment as they sell their home have the equity so it's not about the first time buyers so home sales have been coming down both existing and new but it is still running ahead of the pre-pandemic condition but it is no longer that huge surge and way to fix it is we have to have more supply we need to assure that the builders can build more possibly even utilizing some of the tax credit or existing uh tax benefits like 1031 exchange to produce more a home now i use little jargon but essentially we want to assure that there are incentives to build more homes because this is the only way to assure that the first time buyers see more moderate prices a price leveling out and give some chance for the income to catch up do you view this as a as a bubble at all lawrence i'm not concerned with any price decline because you know when we look at 15 years ago you look at the supply situation where it was over 10 months supply which means that it would take about 10 months to exhaust the inventory today we are at two three months supply extremely tight inventory condition um and even though some of the frenzy activity has now disappeared now that some cases we still have multiple offers but less intense multiple offer situation now so it's really question about the leveling up in the prices and fortunately we don't have those risky subprime lending which is a good thing to assure that housing market is on firm footing and we don't have tanning salon managers managing an inventory of five investment properties i want to get your view on the very long term the secular term let's think out to the end of the decade here we have millennials uh now entering the market as they have been for several years um are we in a secular bull market in housing because i've interviewed several people to say we are in fact we might have some cyclical downturns but over the next 10 years they're seeing prices uh rising for the most part what camp are you in so you know in a short term anything can happen over the short term but over the long haul america is one of the few countries few advanced countries to see population increase last year was one of the slowest population increase of only 2 million but that's still adding to housing demand and we are not even sufficiently supplying to even adequately address 2 million new people but we should anticipate that population will be rising the supply situation is still very restrictive and this will not open up quickly i mean it's going to take several years so someone who's buying a real estate today i think they should feel comfortable that the prices will be certainly higher perhaps it's a more moderate growth in the upcoming years and another factor is inflation anytime consumer price inflation begins to be much higher than normal real estate tends to be a very good hedge against inflation and that's another motivator as to why people are buying curious to know what you see right now perhaps as a catalyst that could serve to really cool this market a little bit is there anything out there that you're seeing that could be a risk uh to the housing market well to its strength i should say the driver for the current activity is the low mortgage rates now pandemic has led to greater demand in the suburbs and smaller town and away from the high density downtown area so we have seen that shift but it's the low interest rate that has really provided so what happens if the interest rate starts to rise uh because anytime inflation becomes stubbornly high that's going to push up long-term interest rates uh and also the budget deficit uh you know clearly the country needs more infrastructure spending for bridges uh the broadband excess but raising the budget deficit could also at some point tip the debt market such that borrowing costs rises so the risk factor is really about higher mortgage rate which will pull back first-time buyers but even in that environment i still see home prices remaining stable because we have this acute housing shortage i wouldn't talk about input crisis uh prices excuse me before we go um specifically the price of lumber we know it's spiked to sixteen hundred dollars crashed five hundred dollars maybe that's off the table but any supply constraints that we're seeing you know with the automakers they can't get their chips are homemakers uh able to get the supplies that they need in order to construct these homes uh you know certainly there was a bit of speculation in the lumber prices that big swing as with the say the cryptocurrency or other meme stocks uh but now the lumber prices appears to have stabilized uh it is still above the pre-pandemic levels the one big concern is the potential number of construction workers right now the builders want to ramp up they're having difficulty trying to find qualified workers so we need to assure that there is a enough people go into vocation training uh infrastructure spending will also take away some of the construction workers into building highways but we also need to think about building more homes because without more home building it could lead to another ramp up in home prices and i don't think we want to see such a rapid increase we just want to see moderate growth in the housing sector all right chief economist with the national association of realtors lawrence yoon thanks so much for joining

videos.title: Housing Market Crash 2021- Here comes the BOOM!
videos.text: is the housing market going to crash in 2021 i am so tired of watching youtubers and all these individuals out there trying to talk about a subject they know absolutely nothing about you know as myself being a top 10 loan officer for the last 16 years i am on the front lines because guess what before house sales close people go ahead and get pre-approved for that loan so i'm at the very front of the line seeing on the front line of the war what's going to happen and i gained a lot of attention last year when i came on social media saying hey guys don't believe it kovit's not going to kill house prices i guarantee you house prices are going to go up and i can't i got hundreds of dms and comments telling me i'm an idiot and guess what i'm not trying to be right in everybody's face but guess what i was right and you know why i was right because data doesn't lie and i'm obsessed with numbers and data i was been a math lead my whole life i was right then and i'm gonna be right about this so i want you guys to follow along with me on a couple things so let's look at the data that just recently came out from the fed so right now there's a total of about 982 thousand homes for sale residential homes for sale all across the country everyone was hoping by may we were going to see a pickup oh all these people were going to start having to sell their houses and all these things were going to happen we're going to see a massive inventory influx zillow just went ahead and put an article out and guess what none of that happened okay so what do i think is going to happen let me look at the numbers and i'm going to let you guys be the judge yourself so right now in total we have 900 000 homes for sale in the united states roughly about a three month supply so in a healthy market you want six months supply of homes now what does that mean that means the amount of homes that are currently listed for sale the amount of buyers in a normal marketplace will take six months to go ahead and acquire all those homes or run out of inventory right so we're at 50 percent of what we need to be for supply and everyone says well brian we have all these people in forbearance in foreclosure when all those things come and hit it's just gonna happen just like it did in 2008 well let me just tell you a couple things on why you're completely wrong let's just look at these two scenarios 2008 40 of all homes purchased in 2008 were investment properties 40 and guess what happens when you run into tough times do you get rid of your primary home or your investment home you get rid of your investment home so we had a massive difference of the type of people that are buying whereas today as of the latest numbers of 2020 that i could find on the type of occupancy that was purchased you're roughly about 10 or of investment properties purchased the rest of the homes that were purchased were either primary homes or a second primary home like you know vacation type home not something you would rent out so we have a completely different segment of types of homes the other thing that happened is guess what the average credit score between these two situations then the average credit score was a 661. the average credit score from fannie mae at the end of 2020 was over a 750. let me just tell you something credit score matters about the quality of a buyer more than anything else i've been in the mortgage industry for 16 years it is 100 true you can call me whichever you want to call me about it but i'm sorry somebody with a 660 credit score is not as credit worthy of a buyer and will not make the same payments on time as somebody that has proven with the 750. it is not lie it is not being judgmental it's a simple fact your credit score shows that you did not make your monthly payments on time and you have a history of doing that a 751 does so we have a much much better borrower at today then guess what happened in 2008 the average loan to value the loan compared to the value of the home was over 80 percent june it is today under 60 so we got 40 equity the average person right now in the united states is sitting with around forty percent equity think about that that's a three hundred thousand dollar loan on an estimated five hundred thousand dollar purchase price whereas back then the average house that that was worth five hundred they owed over four hundred thousand dollars at that time completely different environments now in 2008 we were roughly sitting before all those foreclosures hit we were sitting at a total supply of houses on the market at over a 12-month supply so think about that six months is the perfect average so we already had more houses than we even had or needed to have before all the crazy foreclosures started happening because they over built now what'd they do they screwed up from 2009 to 2019 we built on average of half the houses we needed to build we were averaging about 900 000 single-family homes being built per year between 2009 and 2019 versus over 1.5 million houses during the previous time we screwed up why'd we screw up because builders ran out of money we had a credit crunch you had permitting you had labor issues we had all these things that we didn't build enough houses because we didn't need to because we had this massive supply but guess what happens when you stop building for so long guess what you run into supply constraints and that's what we're dealing with now so just comparing that once again we had lower credit borrowers back then who had less equity far more houses on the market before that even crashed compared to today excellent borrowers tons of equity in their home and we are a three-month supply of houses when we need to be at a six month so now let's bring in your foreclosure question right now as a black knight's most recent data that came out in may right now in 2021 there was approximately 1.8 million first mortgages that are in a what they call a serious delinquency status meaning they believe that they will not be brought current or they will not be able to be pulled out of forbearance so that's 1.8 million now very big change from 2008 to today there is very very strict foreclosure laws very very strict a lender has to do everything they possibly can to make sure that the borrower does not have the ability to repay their mortgage before they even think about foreclosing now question for you of those 1.8 million what did i just establish they're sitting on a lot of equity right a lot of equity two to three times as much equity as back in 2008 so let's say let's play the devil's advocate and let's say of those 1.8 million houses okay let's say a third of them foreclose you know why they get foreclosed when you have that much equity it's beyond me but let's play the devil's advocate because back during the foreclosure period of time of all the homes that went seriously delinquent about 50 of them ended up in foreclosure the other 50 were worked out on modifications so let's use 33 now because it's a good number so what's 33 of 1.8 million that's 600 000 homes okay so 600 000 homes see all those 600 000 homes say they hit the market over the next 90 to 120 days right what do we establish at the beginning of the video we have a what we have a three month supply of houses how many houses do we have on the market right now we have just under a million houses for sale how many do we need approximately in the neighborhood of 1.8 million okay we take 600 thousand houses fall foreclosures and let's say they hit over a three month period of time okay right six hundred thousand houses times nine hundred or add to nine hundred thousand houses we currently have what is that number right that's about one point five one point six million okay what's a six month supply in a stable market 1.8 million houses so we even took every we all happen a third of all the houses that are delinquent all went to and we're a distress sale and they all hit the market we're not even out of six months supply of houses whereas in the foreclosure crisis we were far exceeding a six-month supply before the foreclosures even started now why do i not think we're going to have a housing crash is because one we need houses please i don't need to be mean to people but please if you cannot afford to make payments on your home sell your house we need the inventory we need the inventory and it is not coming then what also people completely forgot that's the difference between now and then as i've talked about before between 1988 and 1996 we had the largest birth rate ever in united history here in the united states guess what somebody born in 1988 is turning today here they're turning 33 years old what happens when somebody's 33 that's the average age of a first-time homebuyer okay so we have next five or six years we have the largest group of people born ever in the united states all reaching first-time home buying age coming after a building shortage we built half as many homes as we should for 10 years of time why we have a period of time where people are now being able to work remote and who's able to work remote the very kids that are reaching first time home buying age i'm telling you from the front lines the vast majority of my clients that are first-time home buyers do not live in the area that they are buying it what i call is a fringe market they're working in la where their job is they don't want to live in downtown l.a they are buying in orange county or in riverside county okay if they are living up in san francisco they can work remote okay they are buying in san diego if they work in san diego and they don't want to be in san diego they are buying in scottsdale and phoenix area okay that is what these people are going ahead and doing so how do we fix the problem okay problem is is people say house prices are getting too expensive yes i do think they're getting more expensive but just depends on your market because riverside county it's not expensive for people in orange county it's not expensive at all so you guys have to understand that we will not see a housing crash in 2021 or 2022. the buyer segment and the quality of buyers does not allow it and the amount of foreclosures even if one third of all the houses that is potentially facing foreclosure all hit the market they all hit within the next 90 days we do not have nearly enough supply okay cool we got enough we got enough supply for three months okay now what happens next so please when you guys are going to be in out doing your housing analysis don't listen for people that aren't into this industry i funded three billion dollars of loans over the last six or the last 16 years i know what's happening i know what i'm talking about where it comes to this now i can't predict the future five years out but i'm telling you right now over the next two years the demand is there to more than engulf any supply that will come from any type of foreclosures or forbearances so you make the judge i've made my answer

videos.title: The 2021 Housing Market Shift Explained
videos.text: hey welcome back it's jason walter here something completely unexpected happened last year as our economy tanked and her unemployment rate increased to double digits in two short months our housing market went absolutely bonkers compared to one year ago home prices in the us have surged by almost 18 but more recently our housing market is showing signs of a potential slowdown so in today's video i'm gonna be sharing five key trends that i'm personally tracking that indicate a shift in our u.s housing market and of course i'm going to be sharing the latest data to help support my conclusion regarding this and towards the end of this video i'm going to be sharing my personal opinion regarding if this slow down is indicative of a long-term trend such as a housing crash or if it's a short-term one and before we get started please hit the like button i greatly appreciate that because it helps support the growth of my youtube channel and it's my goal of reaching 50 000 subscribers by year end and if you're looking to buy or sell a house from virtually anywhere in the u.s then go to home and money dot com slash jason to get connected with a great real estate agent in your neck of the woods the first trend that i'd be sharing today that indicates a housing market slowdown is due to the fact that we have a decrease of mortgage applications for home purchases this is a graphic from the mortgage news daily which pulls data from the mortgage bankers association or the mba so the light pink shaded area is a change in the mba's purchase index as you can see here loan applications for home purchases have been more or less on a downward trajectory since mid-january of this year and more recently mortgage applications for home purchases are behind last year's numbers by 18 a second sign that a real estate market may be slowing down is due to the fact that pending home sales have been declining a pending home sale is when a home seller accepts an offer from a home buyer and his sales agreement is signed according to the national association of realtors or gnar pending home sales decreased 1.8 percent in july and were 8.5 percent lower from one year ago so in addition all regions in the u.s experience a dip of pending home sales from one year ago pendings have now decreased for two consecutive months so what this could mean is that home sales could decrease in the next one to two months because it takes around a month from when an offer is accepted to when the home sale is finalized nar also recently posted that pending home sales have generally been trending downwards since march of this year as home prices have become less affordable a third trend that we're seeing right now is that home price appreciation has continued to cool off based on preliminary data from nar the median home sold price during the past four weeks ending august 29th rose at a slower year-over-year pace of 14.9 that's a slowdown from the 20 price appreciation during the four weeks ended june 13th in fact as you can see here on this graphic sales prices have been on a slowing trend since mid-june of this year on top of this the sale to list price ratio has also tapered down to 99.5 percent which means that homes have been selling for 0.5 percent below the seller's asking price on average in both june and july the ratio was over 100 percent however i feel it's very important to point this out that 99.5 percent for a sale to list price ratio is still very very high this is higher than any other month compared to 2020. a fourth factor that is a sign of a housing market shift is an increase of housing inventory this year so for example housing inventory in the u.s has increased for five consecutive months realtors i also noticed that redfin recently posted that housing inventory has increased since march of this year but more recently has started to flatten despite this the number of houses for sale is still down about 22 percent from a year ago when you compare this year to 2019 it's even a bigger difference though so at this time in 2019 there was 1.1 million homes for sale and now there's less than 700 000 in other words despite an increase of inventory this year it still pales in comparison over the past two years a fifth trend that our real estate market may be slowing down is due to the fact that home tours by real estate agents showing houses to home buyers has been declining according to home tour technology company showing time from january 1st to september 7th of this year home tours by real estate agents were down 1.8 compared to a 24 increase from last year and a 7 rise in 2019. if the number of times that real estate agents show houses to their home buyers if that number actually decreases that could mean that home buyer demand is also decreasing now keep in mind that housing inventory in the us has increased almost 30 percent so far this year so if buyer demand was still super strong one would assume there'd be more home tours this year compared to the prior years but that did not happen because the number of home tours by real estate agents actually decreased so far this year so you may be asking yourself is this the next housing market crash or is this a small blip on the radar let's discuss so what i personally look at is how the trends were seen today how those compared to 2019 which was our last normal housing market i think all of us would agree that 2020 was completely an abnormal real estate market let's first talk about the prices of houses that actually closed escrow or have sold so the red line here represents 2021 and the grade line is for 2019. as you can see here the trends are very similar prices went down in january and february then increased through july now they have more or less plateaued if prices went down for more or less the remainder of the year that would be what we normally would see this is obviously completely different than what happened in 2020 when prices increase from june to the end of the year in addition asking prices of houses listed for sale have also falled very closely to 2019 but there was a very big difference here that i noticed so in mid may of 2019 asking prices dropped fairly dramatically until september however in 2021 which of course is that red line they decreased but not as sharply as we can see very similar trends this year compared to 2019 when looking at pending home sales where pendings actually increase january through june of each year we also can have a look at days on the market which means a time period for when the home is listed for sale until when the seller accepts an offer from a home buyer so days on the market increase in january and february over the past two years and now the trend this year is falling closely to what happened in 2019 so in my opinion what we're seeing right now with a slight cooldown of our housing market is what we normally would see in a normal housing market it's entirely normal for a housing market to settle down for the second half of the year especially as we approach the winter months and the holidays however what's going to be very interesting to see is how the housing market is going to react once the fed begins their tapering of purchasing mortgage-backed securities this is because the fed recently announced that they may start tapering later this year which could cause mortgage interest rates to increase but with a negative august jobs report plus the rise of the delta variant that could delay the fed's plans in doing this please subscribe if you haven't done so already and i'll definitely keep you posted so check out the link below if you want a real estate agent referral from virtually anywhere in the us check out the link below also if you got any value out of this video whatsoever this video literally took me hours to make so i greatly appreciate if you hit the like button thank you so much for doing that also you can follow me on instagram i'm sacramento realtor hope you have an awesome day and i look forward to seeing you on the next video [Music] you

videos.title: Why US Real Estate Is Enjoying an Unprecedented Boom
videos.text: alyssa mccree and her husband planned to move from washington dc to the suburbs when their daughter graduated from high school and just as their daughter was preparing to graduate the pandemic hit and the couple stumbled upon their perfect home this house had been on the market for a month or more when we found it and we really didn't have any competition at that stage because i think everybody was afraid to go look at houses they listed their home for sale in april of 2020 and were prepared to wait for months to find a buyer we accepted an offer on the house after it was on the market for like three days and they offered us more than what we were asking which we couldn't believe i mean we were afraid we weren't gonna sell our house and now we were getting more than what we asked for experts say mccree could not have picked a better time to change houses since april 2020 the housing market in the country has been growing mostly thanks to the record low interest rates only about 2.7 on average in 2020 according to the national association of realtors so they took the advantage of the low and home sales increased 20 percent above the pre-pandemic levels millennials entered the housing competition very actively and they make up roughly 22 percent of u.s population according to the u.s census bureau millennials are the largest generational group in the country and they're aging into their home buying years so they're hitting their mid 30s they want to settle down they want to buy a home that's why the demand went up so rapidly one of the more popular real estate websites zillow registered a 1.5 billion increase in clicks and online views and there are simply not enough houses for everyone who is looking to buy in my estimation of for the country as a whole we are short by 6 million housing units the national association of home builders also acknowledges the housing shortage but their numbers are three times smaller and they add the problem has existed for the last decade and the pandemic only made it obvious the great recession was just absolutely devastating for the housing industry and many just left the labor force for good so we still have a deficit of skilled labor because of that and then the young people are not interested in coming into the construction trade high demand coupled with a shortage of houses caused a spike in housing prices home values in the us have climbed 10 over the past year and homes are selling in a median of 14 days which is 20 days faster than they were a year ago however experts say the housing market is very unlikely to crash like it did in 2008 one of the reasons stricter landing conditions interest rates move up you'll start to see prices flatten out and interest rates should be moving up sometimes in the next six months to a year officials with the national association of realtors believe the market will settle down by 2022 and predict housing prices are unlikely to drop for lisa bocaletza washington nrice miui news
